PixiJS V5 Tutorial (1) — Getting Your First Canvas on Screen
PixiJS is a framework built on top of a JavaScript-based 2D engine. You'll mostly see it used for building 2D web games. So let's do a quick getting-started run and learn the basics
I won't throw a ton of theory at you up front — let's just jump straight into the simplest starter setup.
There are two ways to grab Pixi:

There are already plenty of intros to Pixi out there, so I won't spend too long on "what is Pixi". In short, Pixi is basically a framework that simplifies working with Canvas — same idea as how Vue, React, and Angular simplify working with JavaScript. That said, before reading this post I'd recommend you already have a basic grasp of:
Canvas — not required, but if you already know Canvas you'll pick this up faster.
I already mentioned this in the intro post, but at the start of the series I'll repeat myself a bit anyway.

This whole series is going to center on PixiJS 5.1.3. Pixi's team tends to ship updates like crazy, and even while I was writing this it quietly pushed past 5.1, but the differences are pretty small, so I'm sticking with 5.1.3 throughout.
for a quick and simple intro to the framework. If I get anything wrong along the way, corrections are welcome.

Once it's created, you'll see a black canvas — that means you've successfully built a Canvas with Pixi.
lets you set the canvas height, width, background color, and more.
// default is false; makes fonts smoother (anti-aliasing)
// default is false; transparent background — set true if you want to see the HTML behind it
// default is 0x000000; it's just #000000 written as 0x000000
One property worth calling out here is
. Pixi decides on its own when to use the WebGL render engine versus the Canvas render engine — by default Pixi uses WebGL. If you flip this to

, it forces the Canvas render engine instead. You might be wondering what WebGL even is. In short, WebGL is a JavaScript API that lets the browser render 3D and 2D graphics, which means it taps into the GPU. I'll touch on it a bit more in a later chapter — for now, just knowing this thing exists is enough.
and you want to change the default canvas background color afterward. For that you'd reach for
One thing to watch out for: Pixi colors here are written in hexadecimal.
For width and height, you use
If you want the Canvas to fill the whole screen, use JavaScript's

app.renderer.autoResize = true; // a property to make sure the width and height come out correct
app.renderer.resize(window.innerWidth, window.innerHeight);
// a property to make sure the width and height come out correct
(Side note: if it won't go full-screen, try adding
in your CSS — browsers normally ship with some default styles.)
Checking the PIXI version and current render engine
Finally, you can open your browser's Console devtools to check the current version and which engine is in use.

PixiJS is a framework built on top of a JavaScript-based 2D engine, and it shows up a lot in 2D web game development. In this first post of the series we'll do a quick getting-started run: how to pull in Pixi, how to create a PIXI.Application, how to tweak the canvas size and background, and how to check which version and render engine Pixi is actually using.
